case_name: Agricultural stormwater capture for groundwater recharge with variable pesticide pulses and uncertain trace organics
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for variable stormwater runoff containing alachlor, methomyl, and incompletely characterized trace pollutants to support groundwater recharge with risk-aware treatment selection.

input_data.scenario_background: A managed aquifer recharge project captures stormwater from a mixed agricultural and peri-urban catchment during seasonal runoff events. Monitoring has confirmed episodic alachlor and methomyl detections, but the pesticide profile is incomplete because sampling has mostly occurred after major rain events and some short-duration first-flush peaks were missed. Operators also suspect intermittent co-occurrence of other herbicides, urban hydrocarbons, and dissolved organic matter, while influent quality and flow vary substantially between storms and within single storm events.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Produce recharge water suitable for managed aquifer recharge with strong reduction of suspended solids, pathogens, alachlor, methomyl, and likely co-occurring trace organics, while limiting fouling, excessive by-product formation, and subsurface clogging risk.
secondary_goals: maintain performance under event-driven influent swings
provide a multi-barrier approach appropriate for incomplete contaminant characterization
avoid relying on a single unit process for micropollutant control
limit residuals and energy demand where practical

core_unit_functions.offline_equalization_and_first_flush_div: buffer highly variable storm inflows and isolate the most contaminated first-flush fraction for controlled treatment
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_sedimentati: remove suspended solids, particle-associated organics, and reduce downstream fouling and clogging loads
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration_or_ultrafiltration: polish solids and microbial particulates to protect advanced oxidation and recharge performance
core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_UV_hydrogen_peroxide: oxidize alachlor, methomyl, and some additional unknown trace organics that may not be reliably captured by solids removal alone
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticides and oxidation by-products while providing biologically mediated polishing of biodegradable transformation products
core_unit_functions.engineered_recharge_basin_or_soil_aquife: provide final attenuation, infiltration, and pathogen risk control depending on recharge configuration and regulatory framework

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier train is justified because the source is stormwater with high event-to-event variability and the trace contaminant mixture is only partially characterized
equalization and first-flush management are especially important because pesticide pulses in runoff can be short-lived and significantly higher than routine grab samples suggest
front-end solids control is needed to reduce clogging risk in groundwater recharge systems and to improve reliability of downstream advanced treatment
advanced oxidation followed by BAC is a strong candidate when both known pesticides and uncertain trace organics must be addressed without depending solely on membrane desalination
this train is better aligned with groundwater recharge than RO-heavy approaches because salinity is moderate and brine disposal is not preferred
potential_risks: oxidation performance depends on oxidant demand and may be reduced during high DOC storm events unless dose control is robust
some oxidation by-products could form and require validation, making BAC performance and monitoring important
soil aquifer treatment credit is site-specific and depends on vadose zone characteristics, infiltration rates, and regulatory acceptance
uncertain contaminant peaks may exceed design assumptions if first-flush capture is incomplete
final disinfection needs careful selection to avoid unnecessary disinfection by-product formation under variable organic carbon conditions

core_unit_functions.equalization: moderate hydraulic and quality fluctuations between storm pulses
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove turbidity, suspended solids, and part of hydrophobic contaminant associations
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_or_dual_media_filtration: further reduce particle load and protect adsorption media and recharge surfaces
core_unit_functions.powdered_or_granular_activated_carbon: adsorb alachlor and a portion of methomyl and other suspected trace organics
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: reduce microbial risk prior to recharge where required
core_unit_functions.managed_aquifer_recharge: provide final infiltration and additional subsurface attenuation

applicability_rationale: adsorptive polishing is often practical for stormwater micropollutants when salinity is not the main issue and brine disposal is undesirable
this train is simpler than advanced oxidation plus BAC and may fit medium capex and opex constraints more comfortably
activated carbon offers flexibility when the exact trace organic mixture is uncertain, especially for hydrophobic herbicides such as alachlor
the train still recognizes that recharge applications need solids control before infiltration
potential_risks: methomyl is relatively more polar and may show weaker or less consistent carbon adsorption than alachlor, so breakthrough uncertainty is a key caveat
performance for unknown trace contaminants may vary widely with DOC competition and storm intensity
spent carbon replacement or regeneration requirements may become significant during high-load seasons
without an oxidation step, dissolved pesticides not well adsorbed may persist and require confirmation monitoring

core_unit_functions.equalization: dampen storm-driven variability and reduce peak loading to membranes
core_unit_functions.screening_and_hydrodynamic_separation: remove gross solids and grit to protect membrane systems
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide consistent particulate and microbial polishing
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: reject many dissolved pesticides and some dissolved organic matter to achieve a high-quality recharge feed
core_unit_functions.stabilization_and_disinfection: adjust water quality and manage microbial safety before recharge
core_unit_functions.groundwater_recharge: deliver treated water for infiltration or injection

applicability_rationale: NF can provide a strong barrier for many pesticide molecules and offers more direct dissolved contaminant control than conventional clarification plus carbon alone
this approach may be attractive where recharge criteria are stringent and the operator wants a high-confidence dissolved contaminant barrier
UF ahead of NF helps stabilize membrane operation under variable stormwater solids conditions
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major drawback and conflicts with the stated preference to avoid brine-like residuals
membrane fouling risk is substantial under stormwater variability, especially if first-flush peaks are not well controlled
NF is likely more treatment than necessary for moderate TDS source water and may be difficult to justify economically
uncertain trace pollutant rejection is compound-specific and should not be assumed without validation

core_unit_functions.constructed_wetland_or_bioretention: attenuate solids, some nutrients, and a portion of pesticides through sorption, biodegradation, and hydraulic buffering
core_unit_functions.settling_pond: provide additional solids settling and storm flow moderation

applicability_rationale: nature-based pretreatment can be helpful as a front-end buffer for stormwater and may reduce average contaminant loading at relatively low energy demand
this option may fit sites seeking lower energy and simpler operation with available land area
it can be useful as part of a broader treatment system where runoff variability is large
potential_risks: performance for alachlor and especially methomyl can be highly variable and seasonally dependent, so it is risky as a sole barrier for recharge-quality assurance
unknown trace organics may not be consistently controlled to a level suitable for groundwater recharge
hydraulic short-circuiting and reduced treatment during intense storm events can undermine reliability
this train may not provide sufficient pathogen control or demonstrable micropollutant robustness for stringent recharge approvals

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked baseline because it addresses only basic particulate and microbial control
it may improve clarity and reduce some clogging potential but does not provide a robust barrier for dissolved pesticides or unknown trace organics
potential_risks: insufficient control of dissolved alachlor, methomyl, and suspected co-occurring micropollutants
chlorination is not a reliable substitute for pesticide removal and may create by-products in the presence of stormwater organic matter
high residual clogging and groundwater quality risk remains if recharge relies on this train alone
not appropriate as a standalone solution for the stated recharge objective under uncertainty

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains that combine storm-event buffering, solids removal, and dedicated trace-organic control should rank above simple conventional treatment
solutions recognizing first-flush management and equalization should rank highly because influent variability is central to the case
options avoiding unnecessary desalination and brine generation should generally rank above membrane-concentrate-heavy approaches because TDS is moderate and residuals constraints matter

must_reflect: stormwater runoff requires attention to turbidity, suspended solids, microbial pulses, and infiltration clogging risk before groundwater recharge
known pesticides plus incompletely characterized trace pollutants justify a robust multi-barrier approach rather than reliance on one unit process
recommendations should acknowledge that salinity reduction is not the primary driver in this case
tradeoffs among oxidation, activated carbon, membranes, residuals, and operational complexity should be explicitly discussed

must_contain: why first-flush diversion or equalization matters for episodic pesticide peaks
why solids control is necessary before recharge and before advanced polishing units
why activated carbon and/or advanced oxidation are relevant for alachlor, methomyl, and uncertain co-contaminants
why simple sedimentation-filtration-disinfection alone is insufficient for groundwater recharge under trace-organic uncertainty

should_mention: grab samples may underrepresent short-duration first-flush concentration peaks
compound-specific uncertainty because methomyl may be less readily adsorbed than alachlor under some conditions
oxidation by-product and oxidant-demand uncertainty under variable DOC conditions
recharge basin or soil aquifer treatment performance depends on site hydrogeology and cannot be assumed without validation
membrane fouling and concentrate management risks if NF-like options are proposed
